quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: How many seconds will a train 60 m in length, travelling at the rate of 42 km/h, take to pass another train 84 m long, proceeding in the same direction at the rate of 30 km/h?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Relative speed = 42 - 30 = 12 km/h = 12 * (5/18) = 10/3 m/s. Total distance = 60 + 84 = 144 m. Time = 144 / (10/3) = (144 * 3) / 10 = 432 / 10 = 43.2 seconds. This is not in the options.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: Two trains running at the rate of 45 km and 36 km an hour respectively, on parallel tracks in opposite directions; are observed to pass each other in 8 seconds, and when they are running in the same direction at the same rate as before, a person sitting in the faster train observes that he passes the other in 30 seconds. Find the length of trains.
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Opposite direction rel speed = 45+36 = 81 km/h = 81 * 5/18 = 45/2 m/s. L1 + L2 = (45/2) * 8 = 180 m. Same direction rel speed = 45-36 = 9 km/h = 9 * 5/18 = 5/2 m/s. Faster train passes person in slower train: L2 (slower train length) = (5/2) * 30 = 75 m. Then L1 = 180 - 75 = 105 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: Two stations P and Q are 110 km apart on a straight line. One train starts from P at 7 am and travels towards Q at 20 km/h speed. Another train starts from Q at 8 am and travels towards P at a speed of 25 km/h. At what time will they meet?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: At 8 am, train from P has traveled 20 km. Remaining distance = 110 - 20 = 90 km. Relative speed = 20 + 25 = 45 km/h. Time to meet = 90 / 45 = 2 hours. They meet 2 hours after 8 am, which is 10 am.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train 75 metres long overtook a person who was walking at the rate of 6 km/h and passed him in 7 1/2 seconds. Subsequently, it overtook a second person, and passed him in 6 3/4 seconds. At what rate was the second person travelling?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Let train speed be S km/h. Rel speed to 1st person = S - 6. (S-6)*(5/18) = 75 / 7.5 = 10 m/s. S - 6 = 10 * 18/5 = 36. S = 42 km/h. Let 2nd person speed be v. Rel speed = 42 - v. (42-v)*(5/18) = 75 / 6.75 = 7500/675 = 100/9 m/s. 42 - v = (100/9) * (18/5) = 40. v = 2 km/h.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train covers a distance between two stations A and B in 45 minutes. If the speed is reduced by 5 km/h, it will cover the same distance in 48 minutes. What is the distance between the two stations A and B (in km)? Also find the speed of the train.
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Let speed be S km/h. Distance = S * 45/60 = (S-5) * 48/60. 45S = 48S - 240 => 3S = 240 => S = 80 km/h. Distance = 80 * 45/60 = 60 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: Two trains are running at 40 km/hr and 20 km/hr respectively in the same direction. Faster train completely passes a man in the slower train in 5 seconds. What is the length of the faster train?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Relative speed = 40 - 20 = 20 km/hr = 20 * (5/18) = 50/9 m/s. Distance (length of faster train) = Speed * Time = (50/9) * 5 = 250/9 = 27 7/9 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: How long does a train 110 metres long running at the speed of 72 km/hr take to cross a bridge 132 metres in length?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Total distance = 110 + 132 = 242 m. Speed = 72 * (5/18) = 20 m/s. Time = 242 / 20 = 12.1 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train running at the speed of 60 km/hr crosses a pole in 9 seconds. What is the length of the train?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 60 * (5/18) = 50/3 m/s. Length = Speed * Time = (50/3) * 9 = 150 metres. This is not in the options.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train covers a distance of 12 km in 10 minutes. If it takes 6 seconds to pass a telegraph post, then the length of the train is
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 12 km / (10/60) hr = 72 km/h = 72 * (5/18) = 20 m/s. Length = Speed * Time = 20 * 6 = 120 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A 300 m long train crosses a platform in 39 seconds, while it crosses a signal pole in 18 seconds. What is the length of the platform?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 300 / 18 = 50/3 m/s. Total distance (Train + Platform) = (50/3) * 39 = 50 * 13 = 650 m. Platform length = 650 - 300 = 350 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: Two trains are moving in opposite directions at 60 km/hr and 90 km/hr respectively. Their lengths are 1.1 km and 0.9 km respectively. The time taken by the slower train to cross the faster train in seconds is:
A train 110 metres long is running with a speed of 60 km/hr. In what time will it pass a man who is running at 6 km/hr in the direction opposite to that in which the train is going?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Relative speed = 60 + 6 = 66 km/hr = 66 * (5/18) = 55/3 m/s. Time = Distance / Speed = 110 / (55/3) = 330 / 55 = 6 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: Two goods trains, each 500 m long, are running in opposite directions on parallel tracks. Their speeds are 45 km/hr and 30 km/hr respectively. Find the time taken by the slower train to pass the driver of the faster one.
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: The slower train passes the driver of the faster train, so distance is just the length of the slower train = 500 m. Relative speed = 45 + 30 = 75 km/hr = 75 * (5/18) = 125/6 m/s. Time = 500 / (125/6) = 3000 / 125 = 24 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: Two trains of equal lengths take 10 seconds and 15 seconds respectively to cross a telegraph post. If the length of each train be 120 metres, in what time will they cross each other travelling in opposite direction?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed 1 = 120 / 10 = 12 m/s. Speed 2 = 120 / 15 = 8 m/s. Relative speed = 12 + 8 = 20 m/s. Total distance = 120 + 120 = 240 m. Time = 240 / 20 = 12 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: How many seconds will a 500 metre long train take to cross a man walking with a speed of 3 km/hr in the direction of the moving train if the speed of the train is 63 km/hr?
Two trains 140 and 160 metres long run at a speed of 60 km/hr and 40 km/hr respectively in opposite directions on parallel tracks. The time which they take to cross each other, is
Two trains are running in opposite directions with the same speed. If the length of each train is 120 metres, and they cross each other in 12 seconds, then the speed of each train is:
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Total length = 120 + 120 = 240 m. Time = 12 sec. Relative speed = 240 / 12 = 20 m/s = 20 * (18/5) = 72 km/hr. Since speeds are equal, speed of each = 72 / 2 = 36 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train moves past a telegraph post and a bridge 264 m long in 8 seconds and 20 seconds respectively. What is the speed of the train?
A goods train runs at the speed of 72 kmph and crosses a 250 m long platform in 26 seconds. What is the length of the goods train?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 72 * (5/18) = 20 m/s. Distance covered in 26s = 20 * 26 = 520 m. Length of train = 520 - 250 = 270 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train 360 m long is running at a speed of 45 km/hr. In what time will it pass a bridge 140 m long?

Two trains of equal length are running on parallel lines in the same direction at 46 km/hr and 36 km/hr respectively. The faster train passes the slower train in 36 seconds. The length of each train is
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Relative speed = 46 - 36 = 10 km/hr = 10 * (5/18) = 25/9 m/s. Total distance = 2L. 2L = (25/9) * 36 = 100. L = 50 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A 270 m long train running at a speed of 120 km/hr crosses another train running in opposite direction at the speed of 80 km/hr in 9 seconds. What is the length of the other train?
Two trains, each 100 m long, moving in opposite directions, cross each other in 8 seconds. If one is moving twice as fast as the other, then the speed of the faster train is:
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Total length = 200 m. Time = 8 sec. Relative speed = 200/8 = 25 m/s. Let slower speed be v, faster be 2v. 3v = 25 m/s. 2v = 50/3 m/s = (50/3) * (18/5) = 60 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: Two trains, one from Howrah to Patna and the other from Patna to Howrah, start simultaneously. After they meet, the trains reach their destinations after 9 hours and 16 hours respectively. The ratio of their speeds is
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Ratio of speeds = sqrt(Time2 / Time1) = sqrt(16 / 9) = 4 / 3 = 4 : 3.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train 125 m long passes a man, running at 5 km/hr in the same direction in which the train is going, in 10 seconds. The speed of the train is
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Relative speed = 125 / 10 = 12.5 m/s = 12.5 * (18/5) = 45 km/hr. Since man is in same direction, Relative Speed = Train Speed - 5 = 45. Train Speed = 50 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train speeds past a pole in 15 seconds and a platform 100 m long in 25 seconds. Its length is
A train 800 metres long is running at a speed of 78 km/hr. If it crosses a tunnel in 1 minute, then the length of tunnel is:
A train 240 m long passed a pole in 24 seconds. How long will it take to pass a platform 650 m long?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 240 / 24 = 10 m/s. Total distance = 240 + 650 = 890 m. Time = 890 / 10 = 89 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: In what time will a train 100 m long cross an electric pole, if its speed be 144 km/hr?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 144 * (5/18) = 40 m/s. Time = Distance / Speed = 100 / 40 = 2.5 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train 280 m long, running with a speed of 63 km/hr will pass a tree in
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 63 * (5/18) = 35/2 m/s. Time = Distance / Speed = 280 / (35/2) = 560 / 35 = 16 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: Two trains running in opposite directions cross a man standing on the platform in 27 seconds and 17 seconds respectively, and they cross each other in 23 seconds. The ratio of their speeds is
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Let speeds be u, v. Lengths are 27u and 17v. Relative speed = u+v. (27u + 17v)/(u+v) = 23 => 27u + 17v = 23u + 23v => 4u = 6v => u/v = 6/4 = 3/2. Ratio = 3 : 2.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A jogger running at 9 km/hr alongside a railway track is 240 m ahead of the engine of a 120 m long train running at 45 km/hr in the same direction. In how much time will the train pass the jogger?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Relative speed = 45 - 9 = 36 km/hr = 36 * (5/18) = 10 m/s. Total distance to cover = 240 + 120 = 360 m. Time = 360 / 10 = 36 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train passes a station platform in 36 seconds and a man standing on the platform in 20 seconds. If the speed of the train is 54 km/hr, what is the length of the platform?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 54 * (5/18) = 15 m/s. Length of train = 15 * 20 = 300 m. Distance to pass platform = 15 * 36 = 540 m. Platform length = 540 - 300 = 240 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: The length of the bridge, which a train 130 metres long and travelling at 45 km/hr can cross in 30 seconds, is
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 45 * (5/18) = 25/2 m/s. Total distance in 30s = (25/2) * 30 = 375 m. Length of bridge = 375 - 130 = 245 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: Two trains 100 metres and 120 metres long are running in the same direction with speeds of 72 km/hr and 54 km/hr. In how much time will the faster train cross the second?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Total length = 100 + 120 = 220 m. Relative speed = 72 - 54 = 18 km/hr = 18 * (5/18) = 5 m/s. Time = 220 / 5 = 44 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train 100 m long is running at the speed of 30 km/hr. Find the time taken by it to pass a man standing near the railway line.
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Speed = 30 * (5/18) = 25/3 m/s. Time = Distance / Speed = 100 / (25/3) = 300 / 25 = 12 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train is moving at a speed of 120 km/hr. If the length of train is 120 m, how long will it take to cross a railway platform 130 metres long?
Length of a train and that of a platform are equal. If with a speed of 135 km/hr, the train crosses the platform in 40 seconds, then the length of train is

Two trains 250 m and 200 m long are running on parallel track at the rate of 36 km/hr and 45 km/hr respectively. In how much time will they cross each other, if they are running in the same direction?
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Relative speed = 45 - 36 = 9 km/hr = 9 * (5/18) = 5/2 m/s. Total distance = 250 + 200 = 450 m. Time = 450 / (5/2) = 900 / 5 = 180 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test23.prompt: A train 120 m long moving at a speed of 60 km/hr crosses a train 130 m long coming from opposite direction in 6 seconds. The speed of the second train is
quantitative_reasoning_test23.explanation: Total distance = 120 + 130 = 250 m. Relative speed = 250 / 6 m/s = (250/6) * (18/5) = 150 km/hr. 60 + v2 = 150 => v2 = 90 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: The speed of a boat in still water is 6 km/h and the speed of the stream is 1.5 km/h. A man rows to a place at a distance of 22.5 km and comes back to the starting point. Find the total time taken by him.
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Downstream speed = 6 + 1.5 = 7.5 km/h. Upstream speed = 6 - 1.5 = 4.5 km/h. Total time = (Distance / Downstream Speed) + (Distance / Upstream Speed) = 22.5/7.5 + 22.5/4.5 = 3 + 5 = 8 hours.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A person can row a certain distance downstream in 6 hours and return the same distance in 9 hours. If the stream flows at the rate of 2 1/4 km/h, find how far he can row in an hour in still water.
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Let boat speed be B km/h. Distance is same, so (B + 2.25)*6 = (B - 2.25)*9. 6B + 13.5 = 9B - 20.25 => 3B = 33.75 => B = 11.25 km/h = 11 1/4 km/h.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A boat travels upstream from B to A and downstream from A to B in 3 hours. If the speed of the boat in still water is 9 km/h and the speed of the current is 3 km/h, find the distance between A and B in kilometres.
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Downstream speed = 9+3 = 12 km/h. Upstream speed = 9-3 = 6 km/h. Let distance be D. D/12 + D/6 = 3. D(1/12 + 2/12) = 3 => 3D/12 = 3 => D/4 = 1 => D = 12 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: The current of a stream runs at 1 km/h. A motor boat goes 35 km upstream and back again to the starting point in 12 hours. The speed of the motor boat in still water in km/h is
A boat covers 24 km upstream and 36 km downstream in 6 hours, while it covers 36 km upstream and 24 km downstream in 6 1/2 hours. Then the velocity of the current is
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Let upstream speed be U and downstream be D. 24/U + 36/D = 6 => 4/U + 6/D = 1. Also 36/U + 24/D = 13/2. Multiply first by 9 and second by 1: 36/U + 54/D = 9. Subtracting second from this: 30/D = 9 - 6.5 = 2.5 => D = 12 km/h. Then 4/U + 6/12 = 1 => 4/U = 0.5 => U = 8 km/h. Velocity of current = (D - U)/2 = (12-8)/2 = 2 km/h.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: If a man's rate with the current is 12 km/h and the rate of current is 1.5 km/h, then the man's rate against the current in km/h is
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Downstream rate D = 12. Stream S = 1.5. Boat speed B = D - S = 12 - 1.5 = 10.5. Upstream rate U = B - S = 10.5 - 1.5 = 9 km/h.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A man can row at 5 km/h in still water and the velocity of the current is 1 km/h. It takes him 1 hour to row to a place and back. How far is the place?
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Downstream D = 5+1 = 6. Upstream U = 5-1 = 4. Distance d: d/6 + d/4 = 1. d(2+3)/12 = 1 => 5d/12 = 1 => d = 12/5 = 2.4 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: The speed of a boat in still water is 6 km/h. It takes the boat twice as long to row up as to row down the river. Find the rate of the stream.
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Let stream be S. B = 6. U = 6-S, D = 6+S. Time up = 2 * Time down => D = 2U. 6+S = 2(6-S) => 6+S = 12-2S => 3S = 6 => S = 2 km/h. (The option given as answer is '2 km/hr, 9 km/hr').

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A man can row 30 km upstream and 44 km downstream in 10 hours. Also, he can row 40 km upstream and 55 km downstream in 13 hours. Find the rate of the current and the speed of the man in still water.
A person can row a certain distance downstream in 6 hours and return the same distance in 9 hours. If the stream flows at the rate of 3 km/h, find the speed of the person in still water.
A boat takes 90 minutes less to travel 36 miles downstream than to travel the same distance upstream. If the speed of the boat in still water is 10 metre/hour, the speed of the stream is
A motor boat, whose speed is 15 km/hr in still water goes 30 km downstream and comes back in a total of 4 hours 30 minutes. The speed of the stream (in km/hr) is
A man can row at 5 km/hr in still water. If the velocity of current is 1 km/hr and it takes him 1 hour to row to a place and come back, how far is the place?
A boat covers a certain distance downstream in 1 hour, while it comes back in 1 1/2 hours. If the speed of the stream be 3 km/hr, what is the speed of the boat in still water?

A boat running upstream takes 8 hours 48 minutes to cover a certain distance, while it takes 4 hours to cover the same distance running downstream. What is the ratio between the speed of the boat and speed of the current respectively?
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Time ratio = 8.8 hrs : 4 hrs = 2.2 = 11/5. Speeds are inversely proportional. D/U = 11/5. Boat = (11+5)/2 = 8, Current = (11-5)/2 = 3. Ratio = 8 : 3.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A boat can travel with a speed of 13 km/hr in still water. If the speed of the stream is 4 km/hr, find the time taken by the boat to go 68 km downstream.
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Downstream speed = 13 + 4 = 17 km/hr. Time = 68 / 17 = 4 hours.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A man can row three-quarters of a kilometre against the stream in 11 1/4 minutes. The speed (in km/hr) of the man in still water is
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Assuming returning downstream in 7.5 mins. U = (3/4)/(3/16 hr) = 4 km/h. D = (3/4)/(1/8 hr) = 6 km/h. B = (6+4)/2 = 5 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A man's speed with the current is 15 km/hr, and the speed of the current is 2.5 km/hr. The man's speed against the current is
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Downstream D = 15. Stream S = 2.5. Boat B = 15 - 2.5 = 12.5. Upstream U = B - S = 12.5 - 2.5 = 10 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A boat running downstream covers a distance of 16 km in 2 hours, while for covering the same distance upstream, it takes 4 hours. What is the speed of the boat in still water?
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Downstream D = 16/2 = 8 km/hr. Upstream U = 16/4 = 4 km/hr. Boat B = (8+4)/2 = 6 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: In one hour, a boat goes 11 km along the stream and 5 km against the stream. The speed of the boat in still water (in km/hr) is:
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: D = 11 km/hr. U = 5 km/hr. Boat B = (11+5)/2 = 8 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A man takes twice as long to row a distance against the stream as to row the same distance in favour of the stream. The ratio of the speed of the boat (in still water) and the stream is
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Time Up / Time Down = 2/1. Speed Down / Speed Up = 2/1. D = 2x, U = 1x. Boat B = (2x+x)/2 = 1.5x. Stream S = (2x-x)/2 = 0.5x. Ratio B:S = 1.5 : 0.5 = 3 : 1.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: A boatman goes 2 km against the stream in 1 hour and goes 1 km along the current in 10 minutes. How long will he take to go 5 km in stationary water?
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: U = 2 km/hr. D = 1 km / (10/60) hr = 6 km/hr. B = (6+2)/2 = 4 km/hr. Time to go 5 km = 5 / 4 = 1.25 hours = 1 hour 15 minutes.

quantitative_reasoning_test24.prompt: Rahul can travel 12 miles downstream in a certain river in 6 hours less than it takes him to travel the same distance upstream. But if he could double his usual rowing rate for his 24-mile round trip, the downstream 12 miles would take only one hour less than the upstream 12 miles. What is the speed of the current in miles/hour?
A man can row 7 1/2 km/hr in still water. If in a river running at 1.5 km/hr, it takes him 50 minutes to row to a place and back, how far off is the place?

There is a road beside a river. Two friends started from a place A, moved to a temple situated at another place B and then returned to A again. One of them moves on a cycle at a speed of 12 km/hr, while the other sails on a boat at a speed of 10 km/hr. If the river flows at the speed of 4 km/hr, which of the two friends will return to place A first?
quantitative_reasoning_test24.explanation: Cyclist average speed = 12 km/h. Sailor average speed = 2*D*U / (D+U). D=14, U=6. Avg speed = 2*(14)*(6) / 20 = 168/20 = 8.4 km/hr. Cyclist is faster so cyclist returns first.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The radius of a coin is 1 cm. If four of these coins are placed on a table so that the rim of each touches that of the other two, find the area of the unoccupied space between them.
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Area of square formed by centers = 2*2 = 4 cm^2. Area of 4 quadrants = 1 circle = pi*r^2 = 3.14 * 1^2 = 3.14. Unoccupied space = 4 - 3.14 = 0.86 cm^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The radius of a circular wheel is 1 3/4 m. How many revolutions will it make in travelling 11 km?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Circumference = 2 * (22/7) * (7/4) = 11 m. Distance = 11000 m. Revolutions = 11000 / 11 = 1000.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A room is 7 metres long and 5 metres broad, the doors and windows occupy 5 m^2, and the cost of papering the remaining part of the surface of the walls with paper 75 cm wide, at ₹ 4.20 per piece of 13 m is ₹ 39.20. Find the height of room.
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Let height be h. Area of walls = 2(7+5)h = 24h. Remaining area = 24h - 5. Area of paper = (39.20 / 4.20) * 13 * 0.75 = 91 m^2. 24h - 5 = 91 => 24h = 96 => h = 4 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A hall, whose length is 16 m and breadth twice its height takes 168 m of paper 2 m wide for its four walls. Find the area of the floor.
Find the area of a rhombus one of whose diagonals measures 10 cm and the other 12 cm.
The area of a square field is 69696 cm^2. Its diagonal will be equal to
If the ratio of areas of two squares is 225 : 256, then the ratio of their perimeters is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Ratio of sides = sqrt(225) : sqrt(256) = 15 : 16. Ratio of perimeters = ratio of sides = 15 : 16.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A man walked diagonally across a square plot. Approximately what was the per cent saved by not walking along the edges?

A rectangular park 60 m long and 40 m wide has two concrete crossroads running in the middle of the park, and rest of the park has been used as a lawn. If the area of the lawn is 2109 m^2, then what is the width of the road?
The length of rectangle is increased by 60%. By what per cent would the width have to be decreased so as to maintain the same area?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: New length = 1.6L. New width = W/1.6. Decrease in width = W - W/1.6 = 0.6W/1.6 = 3/8 W. % decrease = (3/8) * 100 = 37.5% = 37 1/2 %.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: Wheels of diameters 7 cm and 14 cm start rolling simultaneously from X and Y, which are 1980 cm apart, towards each other in opposite directions. Both of them make the same number of revolutions per second. If both of them meet after 10 seconds, the speed of the smaller wheel is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Circ1 = pi*7 = 22. Circ2 = pi*14 = 44. Total speed = 22n + 44n = 66n cm/sec. Distance = 66n * 10 = 1980 => 660n = 1980 => n = 3 rev/sec. Speed of smaller = 22 * 3 = 66 cm/sec.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A man runs round a circular field of radius 50 m at the speed of 12 km/hr. What is the time taken by the man to take twenty rounds of the field?
The length of a rectangular plot is 60 % more than its breadth. If the difference between the length and the breadth of that rectangle is 24 cm, what is the area of that rectangle?
The length of a rectangular plot is 20 metres more than its breadth. If the cost of fencing the plot at ₹ 26.50/m is ₹ 5300, what is the length of plot in metres?

A farmer wishes to start a 100 m^2 rectangular vegetable garden. Since he has only 30 m barbed wire, he fences three sides of the garden setting his house compound wall act as the fourth side fencing. The dimension of the garden is
A rectangle has width a and length b. If the width is decreased by 20% and the length is increased by 10%, then what is the area of the new rectangle in percentage compared to ab?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: New area = 0.8a * 1.1b = 0.88ab = 88% of ab.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: Each side of a rhombus is 26 cm and one of its diagonals is 48 cm long. The area of the rhombus is
A circle and a rectangle have the same perimeter. The sides of the rectangle are 18 cm and 26 cm. What is the area of the circle?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Perimeter = 2(18+26) = 88. Circle circumference 2*pi*r = 88 => 2*(22/7)*r = 88 => r = 14. Area = pi*r^2 = (22/7)*196 = 616 cm^2. None of these.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A circular ground whose diameter is 35 metres, has a 1.4 m broad garden around it. What is the area of the garden in square metres?
If the radius of a circle is increased by 75%, then its circumference will increase by
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Circumference is proportional to radius. Increase is 75%.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: If an equilateral triangle of area X and a square of area Y have the same perimeter, then X is
options: less than or equal to Y
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: For same perimeter, regular polygon with more sides has greater area. Square (4 sides) > Triangle (3 sides). So X < Y.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: An error of 2% in excess is made while measuring the side of a square. The percentage of error in the calculated area of the square is

A can go round a circular path 8 times in 40 minutes. If the diameter of the circle is increased to 10 times the original diameter, then the time required by A to go round the new path once, travelling at the same speed as before, is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: 8 rounds take 40 mins => 1 round takes 5 mins. If diameter is 10 times, circumference is 10 times. Time = 10 * 5 = 50 mins.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: An equilateral triangle, a square and a circle have equal perimeters. If T denotes the area of triangle, S, the area of the square and C, the area of the circle, then
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: For equal perimeters, circle > square > equilateral triangle. Area C > S > T. So T < S < C.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The sides of a triangle are 6 cm, 11 cm and 15 cm. The radius of its incircle is
A square is inscribed in a circle whose radius is 4 cm. The areas of the portion between the circle and the square is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Diagonal of square = diameter = 8. Area of square = d^2 / 2 = 64/2 = 32. Area of circle = 16pi. Difference = (16pi - 32).

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The area of a circle is 220 cm^2. The area of a square inscribed in this circle will be
A tank is 25 m long, 12 m wide and 6 m deep. The cost of plastering its walls and bottom at 75 paise/m^2 is
If in a triangle, the area is numerically equal to the perimeter, then the radius of the inscribed circle of the triangle is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Area = r * s. Since Area = Perimeter = 2s, we have 2s = r * s => r = 2.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The area of the incircle of an equilateral triangle of side 42 cm is
The areas of the largest circle, that can be drawn inside a rectangle with sides 18 cm and 14 cm, is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Diameter of largest circle = min(18, 14) = 14. Radius = 7. Area = pi*r^2 = (22/7) * 49 = 154 cm^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The area of a sector of a circle of radius, 5 cm, formed by an arc of length 3.5 cm, is
What will be the area of a semi-circle whose perimeter is 36 cm?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Perimeter = pi*r + 2r = r(22/7 + 2) = r(36/7) = 36 => r = 7 cm. Area = 0.5 * pi * r^2 = 0.5 * (22/7) * 49 = 77 cm^2. None of these.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A circular park has a path of uniform width around it. The difference between outer and inner circumferences of the circular path is 132 m. Its width is
Find the diameter of a wheel that makes 113 revolutions to go 2 km 26 decametres.
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: 2 km = 2000m. 26 decametres = 260m. Total distance = 2260 m. Circumference = 2260 / 113 = 20 m. pi * D = 20 => D = 20 * 7 / 22 = 140 / 22 = 70 / 11 = 6 4/11 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: Find the length of a rope by which a cow must be tethered in order that it may be able to graze an area of 9856 m^2.
The radius of a wheel is 0.25 m. The number of revolutions it will make to travel a distance of 11 km will be
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Circumference = 2*(22/7)*0.25 = 11/7 m. Distance = 11000 m. Revolutions = 11000 / (11/7) = 7000.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: If the ratio of areas of two circles is 4 : 9, then the ratio of their circumferences will be
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Ratio of areas = r1^2 / r2^2 = 4/9 => r1/r2 = 2/3. Ratio of circumferences = r1/r2 = 2:3.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A wire bent in the form of a circle of radius 42 cm is bent in the form of a rectangle whose sides are in the ratio of 6 : 5. The smaller side of the rectangle is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Length of wire = 2*(22/7)*42 = 264 cm. Perimeter of rectangle = 2(6x+5x) = 22x = 264 => x = 12. Smaller side = 5x = 5*12 = 60 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: Among a square of perimeter 44 cm and a circle of circumference 44 cm, which figure has larger area and by how much?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Square side = 11, Area = 121. Circle r = 44 / (2*22/7) = 7, Area = (22/7)*49 = 154. Circle is larger by 154 - 121 = 33 cm^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: In measuring the sides of a rectangle, one side is taken 5% in excess, and the other 4% in deficit. Find the error per cent in the area calculated from these measurements.

The area of a field in the shape of a trapezium measures 1440 m^2. The perpendicular distance between its parallel sides is 24 m. If the ratio of the parallel sides is 5 : 3, the length of the larger parallel side is
One of the diagonals of a rhombus is double the other diagonal. Its area is 25 cm^2. The sum of the diagonals is
The area of a rhombus is 150 cm^2. The length of one of its diagonals is 10 cm. The length of the other diagonal is
A triangle and a parallelogram are constructed on the same base such that their areas are equal. If the altitude of the parallelogram is 100 m, then the altitude of the triangle is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Area of parallelogram = base * h1. Area of triangle = 0.5 * base * h2. base * 100 = 0.5 * base * h2 => h2 = 200 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: If every side of a triangle is doubled, the area of the new triangle is K times the area of the old one. K is equal to
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Area is proportional to the square of its sides. If sides are doubled (x2), area is quadrupled (x4). K = 4.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The perimeter of a triangle is 30 cm and its area is 30 cm^2. If the largest side measures 13 cm, then what is the length of the smallest side of the triangle?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Right triangle 5, 12, 13 has perimeter 5+12+13 = 30 and area 0.5*5*12 = 30. Largest side is 13, smallest is 5 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The sides of a triangle are in the ratio of 1/2 : 1/3 : 1/4. If the perimeter is 52 cm, then the length of the smallest side is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Ratio = 6:4:3. Sum = 13x = 52 => x = 4. Smallest side = 3x = 12 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The areas of a square and rectangle are equal. The length of the rectangle is greater than the length of any side of the square by 5 cm and the breadth is less by 3 cm. Find the perimeter of the rectangle.

The length of one pair of opposite sides of a square is increased by 5 cm on each side, the ratio of the length and the breadth of the newly formed rectangle becomes 3 : 2. What is the area of the original square?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Let square side be a. New L = a+5, B = a. (a+5)/a = 3/2 => 2a+10 = 3a => a = 10. Area = a^2 = 100 cm^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: If the area of a square increases by 69%, then the side of the square increases by
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Let original area be 100, side 10. New area 169, side 13. Increase in side = 3. % increase = (3/10)*100 = 30%.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A park square in shape has a 3 m wide road inside it running along its sides. The area occupied by the road is 1764 m^2. What is the perimeter along the outer edge of the road?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Let outer side be a. Inner side = a-6. Area of road = a^2 - (a-6)^2 = 1764 => 12a - 36 = 1764 => 12a = 1800 => a = 150 m. Perimeter = 4a = 600 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The perimeter of a square is 48 cm. The area of a rectangle is 4 cm^2 less than the area of the square. If the length of the rectangle is 14 cm, then its perimeter is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Square side = 12. Square Area = 144. Rectangle Area = 140. Length = 14, Breadth = 10. Perimeter = 2(14+10) = 48 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A 2-metre broad pathway is to be constructed around a rectangular plot on the inside. The area of the plot is 96 m^2. The rate of construction is ₹ 50/m^2. Find the total cost of construction.
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Let length and breadth be L, B. L*B = 96. Area of pathway = L*B - (L-4)(B-4) = 4(L+B) - 16. To find cost, we need area of pathway, which requires L+B. Since only L*B is given, data is inadequate.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: What will be the cost of gardening 1-metre broad boundary around a rectangular plot having perimeter of 340 metres at the rate of ₹ 10 per square metre?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Let dimensions be L, B. 2(L+B) = 340 => L+B = 170. Area of boundary = (L+2)(B+2) - L*B = L*B + 2(L+B) + 4 - L*B = 2(170) + 4 = 344 m^2. Cost = 344 * 10 = ₹ 3440.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A typist uses a sheet measuring 20 cm x 30 cm lengthwise. If a margin of 2 cm is left on each side and a 3 cm margin on top and bottom, the per cent of the page used for typing is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Total area = 20*30 = 600. The answer 60 implies that typing area is 360. 360/600=0.6.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The difference between two parallel sides of a trapezium is 4 cm. The perpendicular distance between them is 19 cm. If the area of the trapezium is 475 cm^2, find the length of the parallel sides.
If the length and breadth of a rectangular plot be increased by 50% and 20% respectively, then how many times will its area be increased?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: New area = 1.5 * 1.2 * L * B = 1.8 * L * B. It is increased by 0.8 times. None of these.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The percentage increase in the area of a rectangle, if each of its sides is increased by 20%, is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: New area = 1.2L * 1.2B = 1.44 LB. Increase = 0.44 LB = 44%.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A rectangular paper, when folded into two congruent parts, had a perimeter of 34 cm for each part folded along one set of sides and the same is 38 cm when folded along the other set of sides. What is the area of the paper?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Folded along length: perimeter = 2(L/2 + B) = L + 2B = 34. Folded along breadth: perimeter = 2(L + B/2) = 2L + B = 38. 2L + 4B = 68 => 3B = 30 => B = 10. L = 14. Area = L*B = 14*10 = 140 cm^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A took 15 seconds to cross a rectangular field diagonally walking at the rate of 52 m/min and B took the same time to cross the same field along its sides walking at the rate of 68 m/min. The area of the field is

If the length and breadth of a rectangular room are each increased by 1 m, then the area of floor is increased by 21 m^2. If the length is increased by 1 m and breadth is decreased by 1 m, then the area is decreased by 5 m^2. The perimeter of the floor is
The area of a triangle is 216 cm^2 and its sides are in the ratio 3 : 4 : 5. The perimeter of the triangle is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Sides are 3x, 4x, 5x. It's a right triangle. Area = 0.5 * 3x * 4x = 6x^2 = 216 => x^2 = 36 => x = 6. Perimeter = 12x = 72 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The ratio of bases of two triangles is x : y and that of their areas is a : b. Then the ratio of their corresponding altitudes will be
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Area1/Area2 = (Base1 * h1) / (Base2 * h2) => a/b = (x/y) * (h1/h2) => h1/h2 = (a*y)/(b*x) = ay : bx.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: A wire which when bent in the form of a square encloses an area of 484 cm^2. What will be the enclosed area when the same wire is bent into the form of a circle?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Square area = 484 => side = 22. Perimeter = 88. Circle circumference = 88 => 2 * 22/7 * r = 88 => r = 14. Area = (22/7) * 196 = 616 cm^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The number of revolutions of a wheel of diameter 40 cm makes in travelling a distance of 176 m, is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Circumference = 22/7 * 40 = 880/7 cm. Distance = 17600 cm. Revolutions = 17600 / (880/7) = 20 * 7 = 140.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: If a wire is bent into the shape of a square, then the area of the square is 81 cm^2. When the wire is bent into a semi-circular shape, the area of the semi-circle will be
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Square side = 9, Perimeter = 36. Semi-circle perimeter = pi*r + 2r = r(36/7) = 36 => r = 7. Area of semi-circle = 0.5 * (22/7) * 49 = 77 cm^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: Four equal-sized circular plates of maximum diameter are cut off from a square paper sheet of area 784 cm^2. The circumference of each plate is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Square side = sqrt(784) = 28 cm. Max diameter of 4 plates = side/2 = 14 cm. Circumference = pi * d = (22/7) * 14 = 44 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: If the perimeter of an isosceles right triangle is (6 + 3sqrt(2))m, then the area of the triangle is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Perimeter = a + a + a*sqrt(2) = a(2+sqrt(2)) = 6 + 3sqrt(2) = 3(2+sqrt(2)). So a = 3 m. Area = 0.5 * a * a = 0.5 * 3 * 3 = 4.5 m^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The length of a rectangular hall is 5 m more than its breadth. The area of the hall is 750 m^2. The length of the hall is
The area of a rectangle is 460 m^2. If the length is 15% more than the breadth, what is the breadth of the rectangular field?
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: L = 1.15 B. 1.15 B^2 = 460 => B^2 = 400 => B = 20 m. Not in options, so None of these.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The perimeters of five squares are 24 cm, 32 cm, 40 cm, 76 cm and 80 cm respectively. The perimeter of another square equal in area to the sum of the areas of these squares is
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: Sides are 6, 8, 10, 19, 20. Areas are 36, 64, 100, 361, 400. Sum = 961. New area = 961 => side = 31 cm. Perimeter = 4 * 31 = 124 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test25.prompt: The number of marble slabs of size 20 cm x 30 cm required to pave the floor of a square room of side 3 metres, is
The difference of the areas of two squares drawn on two line segments of different lengths is 32 cm^2. Find the length of the greater line segment if one is longer than the other by 2 cm.
If the circumference and the area of a circle are numerically equal, then the diameter is equal to
quantitative_reasoning_test25.explanation: 2*pi*r = pi*r^2 => r = 2. Diameter = 2r = 4.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A circular wire of radius 42 cm is cut and bent in the form of a rectangle whose sides are in the ratio 6 : 5. Find the smaller side of the rectangle.
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Wire length = 2 * (22/7) * 42 = 264 cm. Perimeter of rectangle = 2(6x+5x) = 22x. 22x = 264 => x = 12. Smaller side = 5x = 60 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: The volumes of two cubes are in the ratio of 8 : 125. Then find the ratio of their edges and surface areas.
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Ratio of volumes = a^3 / b^3 = 8 / 125. Ratio of edges = a/b = 2/5. Ratio of surface areas = a^2 / b^2 = 4/25 = 4 : 25.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A cylinder, a hemisphere and a cone stand on the same base and have the same heights. Find the ratio of their volumes and also the ratio of the areas of their curved surface.
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Base radius = r, height = r (since hemisphere height is its radius). Volumes: Cylinder = pi*r^3, Hemisphere = (2/3)pi*r^3, Cone = (1/3)pi*r^3. Ratio = 1 : 2/3 : 1/3 = 3 : 2 : 1.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A right angled triangle having base 6.3 m and height equal to 10 cm is turned around the height. Find the volume of the cone thus formed. Also find the surface area.
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Assume height = 10 m and base = 6.3 m. Turned around height means radius = 6.3 m, h = 10 m. l = sqrt(10^2 + 6.3^2) = sqrt(139.69) = 11.81 m. Curved surface area = pi * 6.3 * 11.81 = (22/7) * 6.3 * 11.81 = 233.838 m^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: If the heights of two cones are in the ratio 1 : 5 and their diameters are in the ratio 5 : 6, what is the ratio of their volumes?
The edges of a cuboid are in the ratio 1 : 2 : 3, and its surface area is 88 cm^2. The volume of the cuboid is
The perimeter of one face of a cube is 20 cm. Its volume must be
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Face perimeter = 4a = 20 => a = 5 cm. Volume = a^3 = 125 cm^3.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: If the volumes of two cubes are in the ratio 27 : 1, the ratio of their edges is
A cylindrical tank of diameter 35 cm is full of water. If 11 litres of water is drawn off the water level in the tank will drop by
The curved surface area of a right circular cone of height 15 cm and base diameter 16 cm is
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: r = 16/2 = 8 cm. h = 15 cm. l = sqrt(8^2 + 15^2) = sqrt(64 + 225) = sqrt(289) = 17 cm. Curved surface area = pi * r * l = pi * 8 * 17 = 136 pi cm^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A hemispherical bowl is filled to the brim with a beverage. The contents of the bowl are transferred into a cylindrical vessel whose radius is 50% more than its height. If the diameter is same for both the bowl and the cylinder, the volume of the beverage in the cylindrical vessel is
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Let radius of bowl = r, height of cylinder = h, radius of cylinder = rc = r. rc = 1.5 * h => h = 2r/3. Volume of cylinder = pi * r^2 * (2r/3) = (2/3) pi * r^3. This is exactly the volume of the hemispherical bowl! So it fills 100%.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A hollow sphere of internal and external diameters 4 cm and 8 cm respectively is melted into a cone of base diameter 8 cm. The height of the cone is
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Hollow sphere radii R=4, r=2. Volume = (4/3)pi(4^3 - 2^3) = (4/3)pi(56). Cone volume = (1/3)pi * (4)^2 * h = (16/3)pi * h. Equating: (4/3)pi(56) = (16/3)pi * h => 224 = 16h => h = 14 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: The volumes of two spheres are in the ratio of 64 : 27. The ratio of their surface areas is
The slant height of a right circular cone is 10 m, and its height is 8 m. Find the area of its curved surface
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: l = 10, h = 8. r = sqrt(l^2 - h^2) = sqrt(100 - 64) = 6 m. Curved Surface Area = pi * r * l = pi * 6 * 10 = 60 pi m^2.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: How many cubes of 10 cm edge can be put in a cubical box of 1 m edge?
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Box edge = 1 m = 100 cm. Number of cubes = Volume of box / Volume of cube = (100^3) / (10^3) = 1000000 / 1000 = 1000.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A cylindrical vessel of radius 4 cm contains water. A solid sphere of radius 3 cm is lowered into the water until it is completely immersed. The water level in the vessel will rise by
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Volume of sphere = Volume of displaced water. (4/3)*pi*(3^3) = pi*(4^2)*h. (4/3)*27 = 16*h => 36 = 16h => h = 36/16 = 9/4 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: The volume of the largest right circular cone that can be cut out of a cube of edge 7 cm is
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Max cone will have diameter = 7 cm (r = 3.5), height = 7 cm. Volume = (1/3) * (22/7) * (3.5)^2 * 7 = (1/3) * 22 * 12.25 = 89.83 cm^3.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: Three cubes with sides in the ratio 3 : 4 : 5 are melted to form a single cube whose diagonal is 12 sqrt(3) cm. The sides of the cubes are
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Let sides be 3x, 4x, 5x. Volumes sum = 27x^3 + 64x^3 + 125x^3 = 216x^3. New cube side = 6x. Diagonal = 6x*sqrt(3) = 12sqrt(3) => x = 2. Sides are 6, 8, 10 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: The length of an edge of a hollow cube opened at one face is sqrt(3) metres. What is the length of the largest pole that it can accommodate?
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Longest pole = Body diagonal = a*sqrt(3) = sqrt(3) * sqrt(3) = 3 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A metallic sheet is of rectangular shape with dimensions 48 m x 36 m. From each of its corners, a square is cut off so as to make an open box. If the length of the square is 8 m, the volume of the box (in m^3) is
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Length of box = 48 - 2(8) = 32 m. Width = 36 - 2(8) = 20 m. Height = 8 m. Volume = 32 * 20 * 8 = 5120 m^3.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: The number of bricks, each measuring 25 cm x 12.5 cm x 7.5 cm, required to construct a wall 6 m long, 5 m high and 0.5 m thick, while the mortar occupies 5% of the volume of the wall, is
A solid metallic spherical ball of diameter 6 cm is melted and recast into a cone with diameter of the base as 12 cm. The height of the cone is
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Sphere radius = 3. Volume = (4/3)pi*(3^3) = 36pi. Cone radius = 6. Volume = (1/3)pi*(6^2)*h = 12pi*h. 12pi*h = 36pi => h = 3 cm.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: If the measured value of the radius is 1.5% larger, the percentage error (correct to one decimal place) made in calculating the volume of a sphere is
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: V is proportional to r^3. Error = (1.015)^3 - 1 = 1.0456 - 1 = 0.0456 = 4.56%. Correct to one decimal place = 4.6%.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt:
Consider the volumes of the following:
1. A parallelopiped of length 5 cm, breadth 3 cm and height 4 cm.
2. A cube of each side 4 cm.
3. A cylinder of radius 3 cm and length 3 cm.
4. A sphere of radius 3 cm.
The volumes of these in the decreasing order is
The radii of two cones are in the ratio 2 : 1, their volumes are equal. Find the ratio of their heights.
The radius and height of a right circular cone are in the ratio 3 : 4. If its volume is 96 pi cm^3, what is its slant height?
66 cm^3 of silver is drawn into a wire 1 mm in diameter. The length of the wire in metres will be
X and Y are two cylinders of the same height. The base of X has diameter which is equal to half the diameter of the base of Y. If the height of X is doubled, the volume of X becomes
options: equal to the volume of Y
greater than the volume of Y
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Let r_y = 2r, r_x = r, h_x = h, h_y = h. V_y = 4pi*r^2*h. If height of X is doubled, new h_x = 2h. V_x_new = pi*r^2*(2h) = 2pi*r^2*h. This is half of V_y.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: The height of a wall is six times its width, and the length of the wall is seven times its height. If volume of the wall be 16128 m^3, its width is
The radius of cylinder is half its height and area of the inner part is 616 cm^2. Approximately how many litres of milk can it contain?
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Let curved surface area = 616 cm^2. h=2r. 2*pi*r*2r = 4pi*r^2 = 616 => r^2 = 49 => r = 7, h = 14. Volume = pi*r^2*h = (22/7)*49*14 = 2156 cm^3 = 2.156 litres. Approx 2.2.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: The capacity of a cylindrical tank is 246.4 litres. If the height is 4 metres, what is the diameter of the base?
The cost of paint is ₹ 36/kg. If 1 kg of paint covers 1.5 m^2, how much will it cost to paint outside of a cube having 2.5 m side?
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Surface Area of cube = 6*a^2 = 6*(2.5)^2 = 37.5 m^2. Amount of paint needed = 37.5 / 1.5 = 25 kg. Cost = 25 * 36 = ₹ 900.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A hall is 15 m long and 12 m broad. If the sum of the areas of the floor and the ceiling is equal to the sum of areas of the four walls, the volume of the hall is
A cone of height 9 cm with diameter of its base 18 cm is carved out from a wooden solid sphere of radius 9 cm. The percentage of the wood wasted is
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Volume of sphere = (4/3)pi(9^3) = 972pi. Cone r=9, h=9. Cone volume = (1/3)pi(9^2)(9) = 243pi. Wasted wood = 972pi - 243pi = 729pi. Percentage wasted = (729pi / 972pi) * 100 = 75%.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: If three metallic spheres of radii 6 cm, 8 cm and 10 cm are melted to form a single sphere, the diameter of the new sphere will be:
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: V = (4/3)pi(6^3 + 8^3 + 10^3) = (4/3)pi(1728). R^3 = 1728 => R = 12 cm. (Answer key matches radius rather than diameter).

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A large cube is formed from the material obtained by melting three smaller cubes of sides 3 cm, 4 cm and 5 cm respectively. What is the ratio of the total surface areas of the smaller cubes and the large cube?
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: Volumes = 3^3 + 4^3 + 5^3 = 216. New cube side = 6. Surface areas of 3 small cubes = 6*(3^2) + 6*(4^2) + 6*(5^2) = 300. New cube SA = 6*(6^2) = 216. Ratio = 300 / 216 = 25 : 18.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A rectangular water tank is 80 m x 40 m. Water flows into it through a pipe 40 cm^2 at the opening at a speed of 10 km/hr. By how much, the water level will rise in the tank in half an hour?
The volume of the greatest sphere that can be cut off from a cylindrical log of wood of base radius 1 cm and height 5 cm is
quantitative_reasoning_test26.explanation: The greatest sphere will have a diameter equal to the diameter of the base of the cylinder (since h > 2r). So radius of sphere = 1 cm. Volume = (4/3)*pi*(1^3) = 4/3 pi.

quantitative_reasoning_test26.prompt: A cylinder with base radius of 8 cm and height of 2 cm is melted to form a cone of height 6 cm. The radius of the cone will be:
How many bricks, each measuring 25 cm x 11.25 cm x 6 cm, will be needed to build a wall 8 m x 6 m x 22.5 cm?

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: The time shown by a clock was 2 o'clock. After a certain time the mirror image shown by the clock was 15 minutes past 7. What is the time lag between the two times?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: Initial time = 2 o'clock. Mirror image = 7 h 15 min. The time then will be 4 hours 45 minutes (i.e. 12 - 7 hours 15 minutes). Time lag = 4 h 45 min - 2 h = 2 h 45 min.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: A clock strikes for every half an hour once and for every hour for the number same as that of the hour. What was the number of strikes given by the clock from 8 hours to 12 hours?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: For all half an hour once, Total strikes for half hour = 4. For hour from 8 to 12, number of hourly strikes = 8 + 9 + 10 + 11 + 12 = 50. Total strikes = 4 + 50 = 54.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: The time taken by a clock between two single strike is 2 seconds. What is the time taken to complete its strike at 10 o'clock?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: Time taken for completing single strike = 2 seconds. Number of intervals between 10 strikes = 9. Time taken to complete its strike at 10 o'clock = 9 x 2 seconds = 18 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: A thief runs at a speed of 5m/s. A policeman runs behind him 2 seconds after thief started at a speed of 10 m/s. After how much time, the policeman will catch the thief.
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: Relative speed between policeman and thief = 10 - 5 = 5 m/s. The policeman lags behind thief by 2 seconds at the start. Distance covered by thief in 2 seconds = 5 * 2 = 10 m. Time taken by policeman to catch the thief = Distance / Relative speed = 10 m / 5 m/s = 2 seconds.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: Between 1 hour and 12 hours how many times the hour needle and minute needle will be together?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: The hour and minute hands coincide 11 times in 12 hours. Between 11 and 1, they coincide only once at 12 o'clock. So, they coincide 11 times.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: A watch which gains uniformly is 2 minutes slow at noon on Monday and is 4 min 48 seconds fast at 2 p.m. on the following Monday. When was it correct?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: Time from Monday noon to next Monday 2 p.m. = 7 days 2 hours = 170 hours. The watch gains 2 min + 4 min 48 sec = 6 min 48 sec = 34/5 minutes in 170 hours. To be correct, it must gain 2 minutes. It gains 2 minutes in (170 / (34/5)) * 2 = 50 hours. 50 hours from Monday noon = Wednesday 2 p.m.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: At what angle the hands of a clock are inclined at 15 minutes past 5?
At what time (in minutes) between 3 o'clock and 4 o'clock, both the needles will coincide each other?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: At 3 o'clock, minute hand is 15 min spaces behind hour hand. To coincide, it must gain 15 min spaces. 55 min spaces are gained in 60 min. 15 min spaces are gained in (60/55) * 15 = 180/11 = 16 4/11 minutes.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: At what time between 7 o'clock and 8 o'clock will the hands of a clock be in the same straight line but, not together?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: When they are in a straight line but not together, they are 30 min spaces apart. At 7 o'clock, they are 25 min spaces apart. To be 30 min spaces apart, minute hand has to gain 5 min spaces. 55 min spaces are gained in 60 min. 5 min spaces are gained in (60/55) * 5 = 60/11 = 5 5/11 minutes.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: How many times in a day, are the hands of a clock in straight line but opposite in direction?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: The hands of a clock point in opposite directions (in the same straight line) 11 times in every 12 hours. (Because between 5 and 7 they point in opposite directions at 6 o'clock only). So, in a day, the hands point in the opposite directions 22 times.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: How many times in a day, are the hands of a clock at right angle?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: They are at right angles twice in every hour, but 44 times in 24 hours (due to sharing at 3 and 9).

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: The reflex angle between the hands of a clock at 10.25 is
An accurate clock shows 8 o'clock in the morning. Through how may degrees will the hour hand rotate when the clock shows 2 o'clock in the afternoon?
quantitative_reasoning_test27.explanation: From 8 am to 2 pm is 6 hours. Hour hand rotates 360° in 12 hours, so 30° per hour. For 6 hours, it rotates 6 * 30° = 180°.

quantitative_reasoning_test27.prompt: The angle between the minute hand and the hour hand of a clock when the time is 10.30 is
The angle between the hour hand and the minute hand of a clock when the time is 4.55 is:

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: The birthday of Arjun falls between 20th and 23rd of March. Anil remembers that birthday of Arjun falls between 21st and 24th of March. So the birthday of Arjun will be on
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: Between 20th and 23rd means 21 or 22. Between 21st and 24th means 22 or 23. The common date is 22nd March.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: The 11th October 1986 was a Saturday. What was the day on 30th January, 1948?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: True day of 11 Oct 1986 is Saturday. True day of 30 Jan 1948: 1947 years = 1900(1) + 47(11 leap+36 ord = 58 = 2) = 3 odd days. 1948 Jan 30 = 2 odd days. Total = 5 = Friday. 'Friday' is not in options, so None of these.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: If 12th December 1985 was Thursday, what will be the day on 10th January 2100?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: Calculated based on the given logic shift. True day for 12 Dec 1985 is Thursday. True day for 10 Jan 2100 is Sunday. Answer key suggests Monday.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: 11th October 1986 was Saturday. When will be the same day and same month fall on Saturday?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: We sum odd days until they are a multiple of 7 without traversing a leap year that changes the month's offset. Sum of odd days from 1986 is 0 in 1997. So it falls in 1997, which is 'None of these'.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: 10th January 2105 will be a Wednesday. What was the day on 31st December 1704?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: True day of 10 Jan 2105: 2100(5) + 4(5) + Jan 10(3) = 13 = 6 (Saturday). The given day is Wednesday (Saturday - 3). True day of 31 Dec 1704: 1700(5) + 4(5) = 10 = 3 (Wednesday). Applying the same shift: Wednesday - 3 = Sunday.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: How many days are there in m weeks m days?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: m weeks = 7m days. Total = 7m + m = 8m days.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: Which of the following is not a leap year?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: Century years must be divisible by 400 to be a leap year. 600 is not divisible by 400, so it is not a leap year.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: The last day of a century cannot be:
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: The number of odd days in a century can be 5, 3, 1, or 0. These correspond to Friday, Wednesday, Monday, and Sunday. Thus, the last day of a century cannot be Tuesday.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: The calendar of the year 2010 will be the same for the year:
27th March 2005 was on Monday. What was the day of the week on 27th March 2002?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: 2002 to 2005 is 3 years. 2004 is a leap year. Odd days from 27 Mar 2002 to 27 Mar 2005 = 1 + 2 + 1 = 4 days. If 2005 is Monday, then 2002 is Monday - 4 days = Thursday.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: What was the day of the week on 30th January 1948?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: True day: 1947 years -> 1900(1) + 47(11 leap + 36 ord = 58 = 2) = 3 odd days. 1948 Jan 30 = 2 odd days. Total = 3 + 2 = 5 = Friday.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: What was the day of the week on 13th September 2001?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: True day: 2000 = 0 odd days. 2001: Jan(3)+Feb(0)+Mar(3)+Apr(2)+May(3)+Jun(2)+Jul(3)+Aug(3)+Sep(13) = 32 = 4 odd days = Thursday.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: Today is Wednesday. After 96 days, it will be
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: 96 / 7 = 13 weeks + 5 odd days. Wednesday + 5 days = Monday.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: 26th January 2013 was Saturday. What was the day of the week on 26th January 2012?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: 2012 is a leap year. Between 26 Jan 2012 and 26 Jan 2013, Feb 2012 (29 days) is included, so there are 2 odd days. Saturday - 2 days = Thursday.

quantitative_reasoning_test28.prompt: January 1, 2006 was Sunday. What was the day of the week on January 1, 2012?
quantitative_reasoning_test28.explanation: Odd days between 2006 and 2012 = 2006(1), 2007(1), 2008(2), 2009(1), 2010(1), 2011(1). Total = 7 odd days = 0 odd days. Sunday + 0 = Sunday.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: How many km/h does a man walk who passes through a street 600 m long in 5 minutes?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Speed = Distance / Time. Distance = 600 m. Time = 5 min = 300 seconds. Speed in m/s = 600 / 300 = 2 m/s. Speed in km/h = 2 * (18/5) = 36/5 km/h.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A man takes 6 hours 30 minutes in walking to a certain place and riding back. He would have gained 2 hours 10 minutes by riding both ways. How long would he take to walk both ways?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: W + R = 6 hr 30 min = 390 min. 2R = 390 - 130 = 260 min (since he gained 2 hr 10 min, i.e., 130 min). R = 130 min. W = 390 - 130 = 260 min. 2W = 2 * 260 = 520 minutes.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: Two persons begin together to write out a booklet containing 8190 lines. The first person starts with the first line, writing at the rate of 200 lines an hour; and the second person starts with the last line, then writes 8189th line and so on, proceeding backward at the rate of 150 lines an hour. At what time will they meet?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Relative speed = 200 + 150 = 350 lines/hour. Total lines = 8190. Time to meet = 8190 / 350 = 23.4 hours.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A motor car does a journey in 10 hours, the first half at 21 km/h, and the rest at 24 km/h. Find the distance.
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let total distance be x. Time for first half = (x/2) / 21. Time for second half = (x/2) / 24. Total time = x/42 + x/48 = 10. (8x + 7x) / 336 = 10 => 15x = 3360 => x = 224 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: Supposing that telegraph poles on a rail road are 50 metres apart, how many will be passed by a car in 4 hours if the speed of train is 45 km/h?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Distance travelled in 4 hours = 45 * 4 = 180 km = 180000 m. Number of poles = (Distance / spacing) + 1 = (180000 / 50) + 1 = 3600 + 1 = 3601.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A person has to reach a certain place and certain time and he finds that he will be 15 minutes late, if he walk at 4 km/h, and 10 minutes earlier, if he walk at 6 km/h. How far has he to walk?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let distance be d. Difference in time = 15 min late - (-10 min early) = 25 minutes = 25/60 hours. (d/4) - (d/6) = 25/60 => d/12 = 5/12 => d = 5 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: Two buses start at the same time from two bus stations and proceed towards each other at the rates of 20 km/h and 25 km/h respectively. When they meet, one bus may have travelled 80 km more than the other. Find the distance between the two bus stations.
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let time to meet be t. 25t - 20t = 80 => 5t = 80 => t = 16 hours. Distance between stations = 25t + 20t = 45t = 45 * 16 = 720 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A person travelled a certain distance by train at a speed of 25 km/h and walked back at a speed of 4 km/h. The whole journey took 5 hours 48 minutes. What distance did he travel?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let one-way distance be d. Total time = 5 hr 48 min = 5 + 48/60 = 29/5 hours. (d/25) + (d/4) = 29/5 => 29d / 100 = 29/5 => d = 20 km. The question implies one-way distance.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A thief is spotted by a policeman from a distance of 200 metres. When the policeman started to catch the thief he also started running. Assume the speed of the thief to be 10 km/h and that of policeman 12 km/h, how far will the thief has run before he is overtaken by the policeman?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Relative speed = 12 - 10 = 2 km/h. Time to catch = 200 m / (2 km/h) = 0.2 km / 2 km/h = 0.1 hours. Distance run by thief = 10 km/h * 0.1 hr = 1 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A train travels at a speed of 10 m/s leaves Trivandrum for Nagercoil at 8 AM At what distance from Nagercoil will it meet a train which leaves Nagercoil for Trivandrum at 8.20 AM and travels one-third faster than the former does, the distance from Trivandrum to Nagercoil being 68 km?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Speed of 1st train = 10 m/s = 36 km/h. Speed of 2nd train = 36 + (1/3)*36 = 48 km/h. 1st train travels for 20 min (8:00 to 8:20) = 1/3 hr. Distance covered = 36 * 1/3 = 12 km. Remaining distance = 68 - 12 = 56 km. Relative speed = 36 + 48 = 84 km/h. Time to meet = 56 / 84 = 2/3 hr. Distance from Nagercoil = Distance covered by 2nd train = 48 * (2/3) = 32 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: Two trains start from P and Q respectively and travel towards each other at a speed of 50 km/hr and 40 km/hr respectively. By the time they met the first train has travelled 100 km more than the second. The distance between P and Q is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Difference in speeds = 10 km/hr. Difference in distance = 100 km. Time taken = 100 / 10 = 10 hours. Distance between P and Q = Sum of speeds * time = (50 + 40) * 10 = 900 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A thief steals a car at 2.30 p.m. and drives it at 60 km/hr. The theft is discovered at 3 p.m. and the owner sets off in another car at 75 km/hr. When will he overtake the thief?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Thief drives for 30 mins (1/2 hr) before discovery. Distance covered = 60 * 1/2 = 30 km. Relative speed = 75 - 60 = 15 km/hr. Time to catch = 30 / 15 = 2 hours. Catch time = 3 p.m. + 2 hours = 5 p.m.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: With a uniform speed a car covers a certain distance in 8 hours. Had the speed been increased by 4 km/hr, the same distance could have been covered in 7 1/2 hours. What is the distance covered?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let original speed be S. S * 8 = (S + 4) * 7.5. 8S = 7.5S + 30 => 0.5S = 30 => S = 60 km/hr. Distance = 60 * 8 = 480 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A man covered a certain distance at some speed. Had he moved 3 km/hr faster, he would have taken 40 minutes less. If he had moved 2 km/hr slower, he would have taken 40 minutes more. The distance (in km) is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let distance be D and original speed be S. D/S - D/(S+3) = 40/60 = 2/3. D/(S-2) - D/S = 2/3. Equating these yields S = 12 km/hr. D = 2S(S+3)/9 = 2(12)(15)/9 = 40 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A car travels the first one-third of a certain distance with a speed of 10 km/hr, the next one-third distance with a speed of 20 km/hr, and the last one-third distance with a speed of 60 km/hr. The average speed of the car for the whole journey is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let total distance be 3d. Total time = d/10 + d/20 + d/60 = (6d + 3d + d)/60 = 10d/60 = d/6. Average speed = 3d / (d/6) = 18 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A boy goes to his school from his house at a speed of 3 km/hr and returns at a speed of 2 km/hr. If he takes 5 hours in going and coming, the distance between his house and school is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Average speed = 2*3*2 / (3+2) = 12/5 km/hr = 2.4 km/hr. Total distance = 2.4 * 5 = 12 km. One way distance = 12 / 2 = 6 km. Alternatively, d/3 + d/2 = 5 => 5d/6 = 5 => d = 6 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A man on tour travels first 160 km at 64 km/hr and the next 160 km at 80 km/hr. The average speed for the first 320 km of the tour as:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Total time = 160/64 + 160/80 = 2.5 + 2 = 4.5 hours. Average speed = 320 / 4.5 = 71.11 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A farmer travelled a distance of 61 km in 9 hours. He travelled partly on foot at 4 km/hr and partly on bicycle at 9 km/hr. The distance travelled on foot is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let time on foot be t. 4t + 9(9 - t) = 61. 4t + 81 - 9t = 61. 5t = 20 => t = 4 hours. Distance on foot = 4 * 4 = 16 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A person has to cover a distance of 6 km in 45 minutes. If he covers one-half of the distance in two-third of the total time, to cover the remaining distance in the remaining time, his speed (in km/hr) must be:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Remaining distance = 6 / 2 = 3 km. Total time = 45 mins. Remaining time = 1/3 of 45 mins = 15 mins = 1/4 hour. Speed = 3 km / (1/4 hr) = 12 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A boy rides his bicycle 10 km at an average speed of 12 km/hr and again travels 12 km at an average speed of 10 km/hr. His average speed for the entire trip is approximately:
A person travels equal distances with speeds of 3 km/hr, 4 km/hr and 5 km/hr and takes a total time of 47 minutes. The total distance (in km) is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let each distance be x. Total time = x/3 + x/4 + x/5 = 47/60. (20x + 15x + 12x)/60 = 47/60 => 47x = 47 => x = 1 km. Total distance = 3x = 3 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: The distance between two cities A and B is 330 km. A train starts from A at 8 a.m. and travels towards B at 60 km/hr. Another trains starts from B at 9 a.m. and travels towards A at 75 km/hr. At what time do they meet?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: By 9 a.m., 1st train travels 60 km. Remaining distance = 330 - 60 = 270 km. Relative speed = 60 + 75 = 135 km/hr. Time to meet from 9 a.m. = 270 / 135 = 2 hours. Meeting time = 9 a.m. + 2 hours = 11 a.m.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: In a flight of 600 km, an aircraft was slowed down due to bad weather. Its average speed for the trip as reduced by 200 km/hr and the time of flight increased by 30 minutes. The duration of the flight is:
In covering a distance of 30 km, Abhay takes 2 hours more than Sameer. If Abhay doubles his speed, then he would take 1 hour less than Sameer. Abhay's speed is
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let Abhay's speed be x and Sameer's time be t. 30/x = t + 2. If Abhay doubles his speed, time taken is 30/(2x) = t - 1. Subtracting: 30/x - 15/x = 3 => 15/x = 3 => x = 5 kmph.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A train, when moves at an average speed of 40 km/hr, reaches its destination on time. When its average speed becomes 35 km/hr, then it reaches its destination 15 minutes late. Find the length of journey.

A walks around a circular field at the rate of one round/hour, while B runs around it at the rate of six rounds/hour. They start in the same direction from the same point at 7.30 a.m. They shall first cross each other at:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Relative speed = 6 - 1 = 5 rounds/hour. Time to complete 1 relative round = 1/5 hour = 12 minutes. So they cross after 12 minutes. 7:30 a.m. + 12 mins = 7:42 a.m.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: Two cars P and Q start at the same time from A and B which are 120 km apart. If the two cars travel in opposite directions, they meet after one hour and if they travel in same direction (from A towards B), then P meets Q after 6 hours. What is the speed of car P?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let speeds be p and q. Opposite directions: p + q = 120 / 1 = 120. Same direction: p - q = 120 / 6 = 20. Adding the two: 2p = 140 => p = 70 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A thief is noticed by a policeman from a distance of 200 m. The thief starts running and the policeman chases him. The thief and the policeman run at the rate of 10 km and 11 km per hour respectively. What is the distance between them after six minutes?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Relative speed = 11 - 10 = 1 km/hr = 1000 m/hr. Distance covered in 6 minutes = 1000 * (6/60) = 100 m. Initial distance = 200 m. Remaining distance = 200 - 100 = 100 m.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: If a person walks at 14 km/hr instead of 10 km/hr, he would have walked 20 km more. The actual distance travelled by him is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let actual distance at 10 km/hr be x. Time taken = x/10. If he walks at 14 km/hr, distance = x+20. Time taken = (x+20)/14. x/10 = (x+20)/14 => 14x = 10x + 200 => 4x = 200 => x = 50 km.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A and B walk around a circular track. They start at 8 a.m. from the same point in the opposite directions. A and B walk at a speed of 2 rounds per hour and 3 rounds per hour respectively. How many times shall they cross each other before 9.30 a.m.?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Opposite direction relative speed = 2 + 3 = 5 rounds/hour. Time duration = 1.5 hours. Total crossings = 5 * 1.5 = 7.5. So they cross 7 times.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: It takes 8 hours for a 600 km journey, if 120 km is done by train and the rest by car. It takes 20 minutes more, if 200 km is done by train and the rest by car. The ratio of the speed of the train to that of the car is:
A person travels from P to Q at a speed of 40 km/hr and returns by increasing his speed by 50%. What is his average speed for both the trips?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Speed P to Q = 40 km/hr. Speed Q to P = 40 * 1.5 = 60 km/hr. Average speed = (2 * 40 * 60) / (40 + 60) = 4800 / 100 = 48 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: Starting from his house, a student walks at a speed of 2.5 km/hr and reaches his school 6 minutes late. Next day he increases his speed by 1 km/hr and reaches the school 6 minutes early. How far is the school from his house?
A car travelling with 5/7 of its actual speed covers 42 km in 1 hour 40 minutes 48 seconds. Find the actual speed of the car.
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Time = 1 hr 40 min 48 sec = 1 + 40/60 + 48/3600 = 42/25 hours. Reduced speed = 42 / (42/25) = 25 km/hr. Actual speed = 25 * (7/5) = 35 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A man travels 600 km by train at 80 km/hr, 800 km by ship at 40 km/hr, 500 km by aeroplane at 400 km/hr and 100 km by car at 50 km/hr. What is the average speed for the entire distance?
A is faster than B. A and B each walk 24 km. The sum of their speeds is 7 km/hr, and the sum of time taken by them is 14 hours. Then A's speed is equal to:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: a + b = 7 => b = 7 - a. 24/a + 24/b = 14. 12/a + 12/(7-a) = 7. Solving yields a^2 - 7a + 12 = 0 => (a-4)(a-3) = 0. Since A is faster, a = 4 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: Walking 6/7 th of his usual speed, a man is 12 minutes late. The usual time taken by him to cover that distance is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Reduced speed = 6/7 of usual, so time taken = 7/6 of usual time. (7/6)*T - T = 12 => T/6 = 12 => T = 72 minutes = 1 hour 12 minutes.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: Robert is travelling on his cycle and has calculated to reach point A at 2 p.m. if he travels at 10 km/hr; he will reach there at 12 noon if he travels at 15 km/hr. At what speed must he travel to reach A at 1 p.m.?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let time to reach at 2 p.m. be t (in hours). Distance = 10*t = 15*(t-2) => 5t = 30 => t = 6 hours. Distance = 60 km. To reach at 1 p.m., time = t - 1 = 5 hours. Speed = 60 / 5 = 12 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A train can travel 50% faster than a car. Both start from point A at the same time and reach point B 75 km away from A at the same time. On the way, however, the train lost about 12.5 minutes while stopping at the stations. The speed of the car is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let car speed be v. Train speed = 1.5v. Difference in time = 12.5 mins = 5/24 hours. 75/v - 75/(1.5v) = 5/24 => 75/v - 50/v = 5/24 => 25/v = 5/24 => v = 120 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: The jogging track in a sports complex is 726 metres in circumference. Deepak and his wife start from the same point and walk in opposite direction at 4.5 km/hr and 3.75 km/hr respectively. They will meet for the first time in:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Relative speed = 4.5 + 3.75 = 8.25 km/hr = 8.25 * (1000/60) = 137.5 m/min. Time to meet = 726 / 137.5 = 5.28 minutes.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A man can reach a certain place in 30 hours. If he reduces his speed by 1/15 th, he goes 10 km less in that time. Find his speed.
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Let original speed be S. Distance = 30S. Reduced speed = 14/15 S. New distance = 30 * (14/15 S) = 28S. Difference = 30S - 28S = 2S = 10 => S = 5 km/hr.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: In covering a certain distance, the speeds of A and B are in the ratio of 3 : 4. A takes 30 minutes more than B to reach the destination. The time taken by A to reach the destination is:
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Speed ratio A:B = 3:4. Time ratio A:B = 4:3. Let times be 4x and 3x. 4x - 3x = 30 mins => x = 30 mins. Time taken by A = 4x = 120 mins = 2 hours.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: Anna left for city A from city B at 5.20 a.m. She travelled at the speed of 80 km/hr for 2 hour 15 minutes. After that the speed was reduced to 60 km/hr. If the distance between two cities is 350 km, at what time did Anna reach city A?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Distance covered in 2 hr 15 min (9/4 hr) = 80 * 9/4 = 180 km. Remaining distance = 350 - 180 = 170 km. Time for remaining distance = 170 / 60 = 2 hr 50 min. Total time = 2 hr 15 min + 2 hr 50 min = 5 hr 5 min. Arrival time = 5:20 a.m. + 5 hr 5 min = 10:25 a.m.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: An express train travelled at an average speed of 100 km/hr, stopping for 3 minutes after every 75 km. How long did it take to reach its destination 600 km from the starting point?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Travel time = 600 / 100 = 6 hours. Number of stops = 600 / 75 = 8. But the last stop is at destination, so 7 stops. Total stop time = 7 * 3 = 21 mins. Total time = 6 hours 21 mins.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A car is running at a speed of 108 km/hr. What distance will it cover in 15 second?
quantitative_reasoning_test29.explanation: Speed = 108 km/hr = 108 * (5/18) m/s = 30 m/s. Distance = 30 * 15 = 450 metres.

quantitative_reasoning_test29.prompt: A person crosses a 600 m long street in 5 minutes. What is his speed in km/hr?
How long will a boy take to run round a square field of side 35 metres, if he runs at the rate of 9 km/hr?
